headline: Gulf War Reignites in One Day
deck: Three commercial tankers were struck around the Strait of Hormuz on 7 July, pushing the UK's maritime warning centre to SEVERE and drawing an American answer of more than 80 strikes on Iranian targets. President Trump declared the interim accord dead before NATO hardened its language on freedom of navigation. Brent crude jumped about 5% as the war-risk premium returned.

art.title: Seven July: Hormuz to Ankara
art.caption: Three tankers were hit in the Strait of Hormuz on 7 July, drawing U.S. retaliation on more than 80 Iranian targets and a SEVERE threat warning · Map: Sprockett, Escalation Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1

body: The Gulf war reignited in a single day on 7 July, when three commercial tankers were struck around the Strait of Hormuz. At 1305 UTC, one vessel transiting the chokepoint was hit by an unknown uncrewed aerial vehicle, suffering minor damage but no casualties [E1]. No party formally claimed responsibility for that strike or the two other incidents the same day, leaving attribution contested and independently unverified [E1].

The cluster of attacks forced the UK Maritime Trade Operations centre to raise the strait's threat level to SEVERE [E1]. U.S. Central Command framed its retaliation explicitly as a response to those tanker attacks, completing a new round of strikes the same day [E2]. Three hits on merchant vessels in one day supplied the immediate cause for more than 80 Iranian targets struck before anyone on the record owned the first drone [E1][E2].

American forces hit air defences, command-and-control nodes, coastal radars, anti-ship missile capabilities, and more than 60 Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps small boats [E2]. Central Command said the operation exceeded prior exchanges in the same theatre and covered the coastal infrastructure that could threaten shipping through Hormuz [E2]. Washington's own tally centred on Iranian military assets rather than reporting new American casualties from any counterfire [E2][E4].

Iran answered with its own account of the night's violence. Tehran claimed retaliatory fire against U.S. facilities in Bahrain and Kuwait [E4]. Those strikes and any damage they caused remain independently unverified beyond Iran's assertion [E4]. The exchange on the record was thus asymmetric in confirmation: a detailed American target list against Iranian claims still awaiting independent corroboration [E2][E4].

President Trump, speaking in Ankara on the eve of the NATO summit, declared the interim accord with Iran "over" [E3]. He called Iran's leadership "scum" and said any resumed fighting would "go very quickly" [E3]. The language buried what had been marketed as a pause in hostilities and replaced it with open-ended confrontation on the same day Central Command finished its retaliatory round [E3][E2].

Alliance leaders meeting in Ankara hardened their summit declaration around freedom of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz [E5]. NATO called on Iran to respect merchant passage through the chokepoint [E5]. The alliance's written text landed the same day Trump buried the interim accord and markets repriced Brent sharply higher [E5][E3][E6]. Diplomatic language followed the gunfire in strict order: tankers hit, threat flags raised, strikes answered, accords pronounced dead [E1][E2][E3][E5].

Markets priced the renewed risk almost immediately. Brent crude futures jumped about 5% to roughly $78 as the war premium re-armed on the attacks and the American retaliation [E6]. Oil climbed back toward levels traders had begun to discount during the pause that Trump now pronounced finished [E6][E3]. The barrel moved before any party formally claimed the tanker strikes, pricing the chokepoint's danger on the sequence alone [E6][E1].

Three tankers, a SEVERE flag, eighty-plus targets, an accord declared over, Brent up five percent toward $78: the day told the story without waiting for anyone to claim the first strike [E1][E2][E3][E6]. Chronology was the indictment.

evidence_box.fragment: struck by an unknown Uncrewed Aerial Vehicle
